title: Someone Else's Keys
summary: An application starts using credentials that belong to someone who left the company months ago. Nobody changed anything.

resolution.root_cause: The EC2 instance running the CI/CD pipeline has AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY environment variables set in /etc/environment. These were configured eight months ago by a former engineer (Raj Patel) for a different project. The access key belongs to IAM user 'raj.patel', whose account was deactivated during offboarding. Because environment variables take precedence over instance profiles in the AWS credential resolution chain, the CLI uses the deactivated credentials instead of the valid instance profile role, resulting in AccessDenied on every API call.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that AWS credential environment variables are overriding the EC2 instance profile
Player uses or references `aws sts get-caller-identity` to discover the CLI is authenticating as the wrong principal
Player proposes removing the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY environment variables from the CI machine
Player demonstrates understanding of the full AWS credential resolution chain order

learning_objectives: The AWS CLI resolves credentials in a fixed order: CLI parameters, environment variables, CLI credentials file, CLI config file, container credentials, instance profile
Environment variables override instance profiles -- hardcoded credentials left on a machine can silently take precedence over IAM roles
`aws sts get-caller-identity` reveals which principal the CLI is actually authenticating as, regardless of what you expect
`aws configure list` shows the source of each credential component, making it easy to spot when env vars are active
sop_steps: Find out who the CLI thinks it is. Run aws sts get-caller-identity on the affected machine. This command answers the question 'which AWS user or role am I acting as right now?' If the answer is a person's user account instead of the machine's role, something is overriding the expected credentials.

Find out where the credentials are coming from. Run aws configure list and look at the Type column. It tells you the source: 'env' means environment variables (settings baked into the shell), 'iam-role' means the machine's own role (called an instance profile), and 'shared-credentials-file' means a file on disk. In this case, 'env' reveals that environment variables are taking priority.

Track down the environment variables. If Type shows 'env', the CLI is reading credentials from shell variables instead of using the machine's built-in role. Search these common locations for AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: /etc/environment, ~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_profile, ~/.profile, and any scheduled job (cron) definitions.
Remove the stale credentials. Delete or comment out the lines containing AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY from whichever file you found them in.
Make the removal take effect. Start a new shell session or reboot the instance. Environment variables persist in memory until the shell that loaded them is closed.
Confirm the fix worked. Run aws sts get-caller-identity again. The output should now show the machine's instance profile role (a role ARN) instead of a personal user account.
Test the deploy. Run the deploy script manually to verify it succeeds with the instance profile credentials.

Audit other machines. Search all other EC2 instances and shared infrastructure (CI machines, jump boxes, dev servers) for hardcoded AWS credentials in environment variables or config files. If one machine had this problem, others might too.

Update the offboarding checklist. Add a step: when someone leaves the company, search all shared infrastructure for credentials belonging to them. Check /etc/environment, ~/.bashrc, ~/.bash_profile, ~/.aws/credentials, and cron job definitions.

Set up automatic detection. Configure CloudTrail (the AWS audit log service) to send alerts when AccessDenied errors come from inactive users or from users that do not match the expected role for a given machine. This catches credential override issues before they cause extended outages.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A credentials file on disk overrides the machine's built-in role
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of environment variables (which the CLI checks second), the CLI finds credentials in a file on disk called ~/.aws/credentials (which it checks third). The result is the same -- the machine's own role (instance profile) is never used -- but aws configure list shows Type as 'shared-credentials-file' instead of 'env'. The file may have been created by someone running aws configure on the machine months ago.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Remove ~/.aws/credentials from shared infrastructure. Use the machine's built-in role (instance profile) exclusively for EC2 workloads so there are no files or variables to manage.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A temporary security token expired but the environment variables are still set
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: An engineer once generated temporary credentials using aws sts get-session-token and saved all three values (access key, secret key, and session token) as environment variables. The session token expired after a few hours, but the variables are still set. The error message says 'ExpiredToken' rather than 'AccessDenied.' The access key may still be valid, but the session token has a fixed lifetime and cannot be renewed once set.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Never save temporary session tokens into persistent files like /etc/environment. Use the machine's built-in role (instance profile) for automated workloads -- it handles token renewal automatically.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The machine's role does not have the right permissions
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: There are no stale credentials overriding anything. The CLI correctly authenticates as the machine's role (aws sts get-caller-identity shows a role ARN, not a user). But the role's permission policy does not include the actions the deploy script needs (like uploading to S3 or updating a Lambda function). The fix is updating the role's permissions, not removing credential overrides.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Define instance profile roles and their permissions using infrastructure-as-code. Review role permissions whenever deploy requirements change.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The instance's metadata service is locked down and the CLI cannot fetch credentials from it
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The machine was configured to require a newer, more secure version of the metadata service (called IMDSv2), but the installed CLI or SDK version does not support it. The CLI reaches the instance profile step in the credential chain but fails to retrieve the credentials. The error says 'Unable to locate credentials' rather than 'AccessDenied.'
related_failure_modes.prevention: Update the AWS CLI and SDKs to versions that support IMDSv2 before enabling the requirement on instances. Test credential retrieval after making the switch.

sop_practices: Use the machine's built-in role (called an instance profile) instead of hardcoded access keys for any workload running on EC2. Instance profiles provide temporary credentials that rotate automatically -- there are no keys to lose, leak, or forget to remove when someone leaves the company.

When you see an AccessDenied error, the very first thing to check is who the CLI thinks it is. Run aws sts get-caller-identity. If the answer is a personal user account instead of the machine's role, something in the credential chain is overriding the expected credentials.

Add a credential sweep to your offboarding checklist. When someone leaves the company, search all shared infrastructure (CI machines, jump boxes, dev servers) for their credentials in environment variables, credentials files (~/.aws/credentials), config files, and cron job definitions.

Set up automatic detection using CloudTrail (the AWS audit log service). Configure alerts for AccessDenied errors from inactive users or from users that do not match the expected role for a given machine. This catches credential override problems before they cause days-long outages.

glossary.IAM (Identity and Access Management): The AWS service that controls who can do what. You create users, roles, and policies here to grant or deny access to other AWS services.
glossary.IAM Role: A set of permissions that can be assumed by a user, service, or application. Unlike a user, a role has no permanent credentials -- it issues temporary ones.
glossary.Instance Profile: A wrapper that lets an EC2 instance assume an IAM role. When attached, the instance can call AWS APIs using temporary credentials without any hardcoded keys.
glossary.Access Key: A pair of values (an access key ID and a secret access key) that identify an IAM user or role for programmatic access. Long-lived access keys are a security risk if not rotated.
glossary.STS (Security Token Service): The AWS service that issues temporary, limited-privilege credentials. Used behind the scenes when you assume a role or use an instance profile.
glossary.Credential Provider Chain: The fixed order in which the AWS CLI and SDKs look for credentials. The first source found wins. Environment variables come before instance profiles in this order.
glossary.Environment Variable: A key-value pair set in the operating system shell. AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY are environment variables the CLI checks early in the credential chain.
glossary.GetCallerIdentity: An STS API call that returns the IAM principal (user or role) the CLI is currently authenticating as. It works even when the principal lacks permissions for anything else.

components.name: EC2 instance fenwick-ci-01
components.role: Runs the CI/CD deploy script that calls S3, Secrets Manager, and Lambda APIs
components.failure_impact: If credentials resolve to an inactive user, every AWS API call returns AccessDenied and deploys fail

components.name: Instance profile fenwick-ci-deploy
components.role: Provides temporary credentials via IAM role fenwick-ci-deploy-role with correct permissions for the deploy pipeline
components.failure_impact: If overridden by higher-priority credentials in the chain, the instance profile is never used

components.name: Environment variables AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY in /etc/environment
components.role: Hardcoded credentials set eight months ago by a former engineer, taking priority over the instance profile in the credential chain
components.failure_impact: These deactivated credentials override the valid instance profile, causing all API calls to fail with AccessDenied

components.name: IAM user raj.patel (deactivated, access key inactive)
components.role: Former employee whose access keys were deactivated during offboarding
components.failure_impact: Deactivated keys cause AccessDenied for any API call authenticated with them

components.name: S3 bucket fenwick-deploy-artifacts
components.role: Stores deployment artifacts uploaded by the CI/CD pipeline
components.failure_impact: If the deploy script cannot authenticate, artifact uploads fail and the release is blocked

components.name: Secrets Manager secret /prod/db-credentials
components.role: Stores production database credentials retrieved during deployment
components.failure_impact: If GetSecretValue fails, the deploy cannot configure database access for the application

components.name: Lambda function fenwick-invoice-processor
components.role: Invoice processing function updated by the CI/CD deploy pipeline
components.failure_impact: If UpdateFunctionCode fails, the invoice processor runs stale code

system.data_flow: Deploy script runs on EC2 instance. CLI checks credential chain: finds environment variables (priority 2) before instance profile (priority 6). Environment variables point to raj.patel's inactive access key. Every API call (S3 PutObject, Secrets Manager GetSecretValue, Lambda UpdateFunctionCode) authenticates as raj.patel and returns AccessDenied.
system.what_broke: Environment variables containing a former employee's access keys were set on the CI machine eight months ago and never cleaned up. The keys were deactivated during offboarding. Because environment variables outrank instance profiles in the credential chain, the CLI never reaches the valid instance profile. A recent CLI update changed how the credential chain resolved, making the environment variables consistently take precedence.

progressive_clues: who does the pipeline think it is?
run aws sts get-caller-identity on the CI machine
check if there are AWS credential environment variables set on the instance
environment variables take precedence over instance profiles in the credential chain
